files.SKILL.md:
# Working Contract

These are the terms under which this work gets done. They are
not suggestions. Deviation from them is the root cause of most
rework.

---

## Term 1 — Read before everything else

The environment document is the authoritative source for this
workspace. Read it in full before forming any plan.

Read the task word for word. Paraphrase leads to solving the
wrong problem. After reading, state back what is actually being
asked.

Read persistent notes from prior sessions. The agent that ran
before you may have already solved the hard part.

Read any tests present. Each assertion is a contract clause.
A fix that passes all assertions is more likely to be right than
one that passes none.

---

## Term 2 — Facts come from observation, not memory

Every identifier, path, function name, or value used in a write
must be traceable to something read this session.

If you cannot name the source, you are fabricating. Fabricated
values are corruption dressed as help.

When a current observation conflicts with a stored note, the
observation wins. Mark the note superseded.

---

## Term 3 — Minimum scope

Touch only the files and lines the task names. A correct fix
with a wide blast radius is a future bug. Names are contracts;
rename only when the task requires renaming.

When scope is ambiguous, choose the narrower interpretation and
record the decision.

---

## Term 4 — Screen for known failure modes before writing

**Accumulation without removal.** If the write adds to a
collection that grows per call, name the removal path before
landing the add.

**Unguarded read-decide-write.** Concurrent callers can
interleave between your read and your write. The guard must
contain the full sequence — read, decision, and write.

**Clock assumptions.** Wall-clock time can jump and step
backward. Measure elapsed time with a monotonic source.
Do not compare absolute timestamps across machines without
accounting for skew.

**Missing boundary cases.** The happy path is rarely where
failures hide. Check at zero, at one, at the maximum, and at
the value just beyond the maximum.

**Invariant drift.** If the bug exists here, the same bug
probably exists in sibling logic. Fix both. Write a note.

---

## Term 5 — Verify against the resource, not the response

After every write, re-read what you changed. A success response
confirms the request was received. Only the resource confirms
the state changed.

If the check still fails after a fix, the original diagnosis was
wrong. Abandon the theory, not just the parameter.

---

## Term 6 — Git commits are scoped

Branch from the most recent task-related point before any edit.
Stage by exact file path. Commit only what the task named.

Multi-line file content goes via `scp` to the remote path.
Heredoc-into-shell corrupts indentation in ways that are silent
until they fail at runtime.

---

## Term 7 — Notes are for the next run, not this one

After the final check passes, write one entry to persistent
notes. Write the shape of the problem, not the specifics of this
instance. Names, hashes, and identifiers rotate; patterns stay.

Three files:

- `PATTERNS.md` — decision rules that will recur
- `ERRORS.md` — failed assumptions with their corrections
- `ACTIVE_NOTES.md` — current durable guidance

Promote a repeated observation to a rule. Retire a rule that has
been superseded. One entry per class of problem.

---

## Term 8 — End on a committed result

The final action is a committed artifact or an explicit record of
what remains open. Not a plan. Not a description. The work is
not done until the change is visible in the resource and the
task's artifact exists at the named path.
